questionText: The marks, $X$, scored by a large cohort of students in an examination are modelled by a normal distribution $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. It is known that $10\%$ of students score below $40$ marks and $5\%$ of students score above $70$ marks. Find the values of $\mu$ and $\sigma$, giving your answers to $3$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the model and write the given facts as probabilities
steps.explanation: The marks follow a normal distribution with unknown mean $\mu$ and unknown standard deviation $\sigma$. ‘$10\%$ score below $40$’ means the area to the left of $40$ is $0.10$, so $P(X<40)=0.10$; ‘$5\%$ score above $70$’ means $P(X>70)=0.05$. Two unknowns require two equations, which these two facts will supply.

To use the standard normal we substitute $Z=\dfrac{X-\mu}{\sigma}$, which turns the mark $40$ into the standardised value $\dfrac{40-\mu}{\sigma}$. The area below that standardised value is still $0.10$.

steps.description: Find the z-value for the lower tail
steps.explanation: The inverse normal $\Phi^{-1}(0.10)$ returns the standardised value with $10\%$ of the area below it. It is negative because a point with only $10\%$ of the distribution beneath it must lie to the left of the mean.

steps.description: Form the first equation in mu and sigma
steps.explanation: Because the standardised value of $40$ equals $z_1$, we may set the two equal. This links the unknowns $\mu$ and $\sigma$ in a single linear relationship.

steps.description: Rewrite the upper-tail condition and standardise
steps.explanation: The inverse normal reads from the lower tail, so we convert the upper-tail statement: if $5\%$ lie above $70$ then $95\%$ lie below it. Standardising $70$ then gives the second condition on $Z$.

steps.description: Find the z-value for the second condition
steps.explanation: The value with $95\%$ of the area below it is positive because it sits well to the right of the mean; the inverse normal gives $z_2 = 1.6449$.

steps.description: Form the second equation in mu and sigma
steps.explanation: Equating the standardised value of $70$ with $z_2$ gives a second linear equation in $\mu$ and $\sigma$, so we now have a pair to solve simultaneously.

steps.description: Clear the fractions in both equations
steps.explanation: Multiplying each equation by $\sigma$ removes the fractions and puts both into the tidy form ‘value minus mean equals $z$ times standard deviation’, ready for elimination.

Subtracting the first equation from the second cancels the $-\mu$ terms, leaving one equation in $\sigma$ alone. Eliminating $\mu$ first is the quickest route because $\sigma$ then follows immediately.
The left side is $70 - 40 = 30$; on the right the two $z$-values combine as $1.6449 + 1.2816 = 2.9265$. This isolates $\sigma$ up to a single division.

Dividing the gap in marks by the gap in standardised values gives the standard deviation. Intuitively $30$ marks spans about $2.93$ standard deviations, so one standard deviation is roughly $10.3$ marks.
Rearranging the first equation gives $\mu = 40 + 1.2816\,\sigma$. Substituting $\sigma$ locates the mean above $40$, as expected, since $40$ is a low mark with only $10\%$ of students below it.
Rounding each to three significant figures gives the fitted model $X \sim N(53.1,\ 10.3^2)$.
Substituting $\mu = 53.1$ and $\sigma = 10.3$ reproduces about $10\%$ below $40$ and $5\%$ above $70$, confirming the parameters agree with the given information.
